Summary of the Query:
The query aims to find the father of a scientist at MIT who won the Queen Elizabeth Prize for Engineering in 2013.

Skills and Tools:
1. Research and Information Analysis
2. Web Search

Explanations:
1. Research and Information Analysis: This skill is necessary to understand the context and break down the query into manageable parts. It involves identifying the key elements (scientist, MIT, Queen Elizabeth Prize for Engineering, 2013) and determining the required information (father's name).
2. Web Search: Tools like Ground_Google_Search_Tool, Web_RAG_Search_Tool, and Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool are essential for finding the relevant information. They can help identify the winning scientist and, subsequently, find information about their father.

Additional Considerations:
- The query requires multiple steps: first identifying the scientist who won the prize, and then finding information about their father.
- Verification of the information from multiple sources is essential to ensure accuracy.
- The use of specific queries and tools like Wikipedia_RAG_Search_Tool can help in narrowing down the search and obtaining reliable information.
</answer>

result:
Sir Tim Berners-Lee, the 3COM Founders Professor of Engineering at MIT, was one of the recipients of the inaugural Queen Elizabeth Prize for Engineering in 2013.[1](https://news.mit.edu/2013/berners-lee-wins-first-queen-elizabeth-prize-0318) He was recognized for his pivotal role in creating the World Wide Web.[1](https://news.mit.edu/2013/berners-lee-wins-first-queen-elizabeth-prize-0318), [2](https://qeprize.org/winners/the-internet-and-the-web), [3](https://www.w3.org/news/2013/tim-berners-lee-marc-andreessen-vinton-cerf-robert-kahn-and-louis-pouzin-awarded-2013-queen-elizabet/)

The 2013 prize was shared among five engineers for their groundbreaking contributions to the Internet and the World Wide Web. The other recipients were Robert Kahn, Vinton Cerf, Louis Pouzin, and Marc Andreessen.[4](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Queen_Elizabeth_Prize_for_Engineering), [5](https://www.gov.uk/government/news/winners-of-first-queen-elizabeth-prize-for-engineering-announced), [6](https://raeng.org.uk/programmes-and-prizes/prizes/queen-elizabeth-prize-for-engineering/), [2](https://qeprize.org/winners/the-internet-and-the-web), [3](https://www.w3.org/news/2013/tim-berners-lee-marc-andreessen-vinton-cerf-robert-kahn-and-louis-pouzin-awarded-2013-queen-elizabet/)
